era_context: The late Renaissance and early modern period. The Council of Trent is in its final sessions (concluding in 1563), establishing the framework for the Catholic Counter-Reformation. The Ottoman Empire under Süleyman the Magnificent remains dominant in the Mediterranean and Near East. Spain under Philip II is consolidating global empire. In East Asia, the Ming Dynasty rules China; Japan is fragmented during the Sengoku period. The Age of Exploration is well underway, with European powers competing for colonial territories and trade routes. The French Wars of Religion are beginning.

events.title: First War of Religion in France (Massacre of Vassy)
events.description: The First War of Religion in France began in March 1562 with the Massacre of Vassy, in which Catholic forces under the Duke of Guise attacked a Huguenot (Protestant) congregation, killing approximately 600 people. The massacre sparked the first of the French Wars of Religion, a series of civil conflicts between Catholics and Huguenots that would last for decades. The massacre was a turning point in French religious conflict, transforming religious tensions into open warfare. The First War of Religion lasted until the Edict of Amboise in 1563. The conflict is significant as a major moment in the European Wars of Religion and as a demonstration of the deep religious divisions in 16th-century France.
key_figures: Louis I, Prince of Condé (Huguenot leader)
Catherine de Medici (Queen Mother of France)

sources.name: Contemporary accounts by Huguenot and Catholic chroniclers
Historiographic consensus on the French Wars of Religion

events.certainty_note: The Massacre of Vassy in March 1562 is well-documented in contemporary sources; the date and basic facts are secure.
cross_references: 1563-003 (Edict of Amboise, ending the First War of Religion)

events.title: John Hawkins's First Slave Trading Voyage
events.description: English merchant John Hawkins undertook his first slave trading voyage in 1562, sailing to West Africa to acquire enslaved Africans and then to the Caribbean to sell them to Spanish colonists. Hawkins's voyage marked the beginning of English involvement in the transatlantic slave trade. The voyage was profitable and encouraged further English participation in the slave trade. Hawkins's voyages are significant as early examples of English colonial and commercial expansion and as part of the broader history of the transatlantic slave trade. The voyage is also notable for its role in English-Spanish tensions, as Spain claimed monopoly over trade in the Caribbean.
key_figures: John Hawkins (English merchant and slave trader)
Queen Elizabeth I (who invested in the voyage)

sources.name: Contemporary accounts and records of Hawkins's voyage

events.certainty_note: Hawkins's first slave trading voyage in 1562 is well-documented in contemporary accounts and later compilations.
cross_references: 1568-001 (Hawkins's third voyage and the Battle of San Juan de Ulúa)

events.title: Edict of Saint-Germain (Provisional Peace in France)
events.description: The Edict of Saint-Germain, issued in January 1562, was an attempt by the French crown to prevent religious conflict by granting limited rights to Protestants. However, the edict was quickly superseded by the outbreak of the First War of Religion following the Massacre of Vassy in March 1562. The edict is significant as an early attempt at religious coexistence in France, though it proved ineffective in preventing conflict.
key_figures: Catherine de Medici (Queen Mother of France)
events.certainty_note: The Edict of Saint-Germain is well-documented in primary sources; the date (January 1562) is secure.

events.title: Akbar's Military Campaigns in Northern India
events.description: The Mughal Emperor Akbar continued his military campaigns to consolidate control over northern India in 1562. Following his earlier victories and the death of his regent Bairam Khan in 1561, Akbar was consolidating Mughal power through military conquest and administrative reform. The year 1562 saw continued expansion of Mughal territory and influence in the Gangetic plain and surrounding regions.

sources.name: Akbarnama by Abul Fazl (later compilation based on contemporary records)

events.certainty_note: Akbar's military campaigns in 1562 are documented in later Mughal chronicles; specific events and dates are less precisely recorded than major sieges.

events.title: Spanish Conquest of Nueva Cádiz (Margarita Island)
events.description: Spanish forces consolidated control over Nueva Cádiz on Margarita Island in the Caribbean in 1562. The island was significant for its pearl fisheries and strategic location. The Spanish conquest and settlement of Margarita Island was part of the broader Spanish colonization of the Caribbean and northern South America. The island remained under Spanish control and was an important center of pearl production.
events.certainty_note: The Spanish consolidation of control over Margarita Island is documented in colonial records; the exact date (1562) may be approximate (±1–2 years).

disconfirming_evidence: The Massacre of Vassy is often presented as a spontaneous outbreak of religious violence, but modern scholarship (particularly by Natalie Zemon Davis and others) emphasizes the role of organized leadership and suggests that the massacre was partly a deliberate political act by the Duke of Guise to provoke conflict. Additionally, the death toll of the massacre (often cited as 600) is uncertain and may be exaggerated in contemporary accounts. John Hawkins's slave trading voyages are sometimes presented as a straightforward commercial enterprise, but they were also part of a broader pattern of English colonial expansion and were controversial even at the time. The Edict of Saint-Germain is sometimes presented as a generous grant of religious toleration, but it actually granted very limited rights to Protestants and was quickly superseded by conflict.
historiographic_note: Documentation for 1562 is rich for European events (the First War of Religion, the Edict of Saint-Germain, Hawkins's slave trading voyage) but less detailed for Asia and the Americas. The Massacre of Vassy is extensively documented in contemporary chronicles and later accounts. The Edict of Saint-Germain is well-documented in primary sources. Hawkins's slave trading voyage is documented in contemporary accounts and later compilations. Akbar's military campaigns are documented in later Mughal chronicles but with less precision regarding specific events in 1562. The Spanish conquest of Margarita Island is documented in colonial records but with some uncertainty regarding the exact date. Overall, 1562 reflects the documentary bias toward European and Mediterranean history, with particular emphasis on religious and political conflicts.

graph_edges.note: The First War of Religion (beginning with the Massacre of Vassy in 1562) led to the Edict of Amboise in 1563.
Hawkins's first slave trading voyage in 1562 was followed by subsequent voyages, including his third voyage in 1568.
The Edict of Saint-Germain (January 1562) attempted to prevent religious conflict, but the Massacre of Vassy (March 1562) sparked the First War of Religion.
